description:
You are a storage-administrator who has just inherited responsibility for a small data-collection area that lives in:

/home/user/storage_samples

Your first job is to perform a quick-and-dirty space audit and leave a short, parsable diagnostic report that other scripts can consume later.  
Carry out the following:

1. Calculate the **total number of bytes** currently consumed by the whole directory tree rooted at /home/user/storage_samples (the number must be an *exact* byte count, not a human-readable approximation).  
2. Identify every **regular file whose size is strictly greater than 1 MiB (1 048 576 bytes)** within that same tree.  
   • Report each such file **relative to /home/user/storage_samples** (e.g. `images/photo2.jpg`, not `/home/user/storage_samples/images/photo2.jpg`).  
   • Sort this list in descending order by size (largest file first).  
3. Store the results in the log file

/home/user/storage_audit/disk_space.log

Make sure the directory /home/user/storage_audit exists (create it if necessary) and that the file is world-readable (chmod 644 or looser).

Exactly match the following output format in disk_space.log (the automated tester is case- and whitespace-sensitive):

────────────────────────
TOTAL_BYTES=<integer>

LARGE_FILES (>1MB):
<relative_path>,<size_in_bytes>
<relative_path>,<size_in_bytes>
…
────────────────────────

Requirements for disk_space.log:
• Line-1 must start with the literal text `TOTAL_BYTES=` immediately followed by the integer value.  
• Line-2 must be completely blank.  
• Line-3 must be the header `LARGE_FILES (>1MB):` exactly as shown.  
• Every subsequent line must contain a large file entry consisting of the relative path, a comma, **no spaces**, and the exact byte size.  
• The file must end with a single trailing newline and no extra blank lines beyond the required one after TOTAL_BYTES.  
• Do not include any files whose size is *exactly* 1 048 576 bytes—only those that are **larger**.

Your task is finished when:
• /home/user/storage_audit/disk_space.log exists with the exact format above,  
• The TOTAL_BYTES value is correct, and  
• The list of large files and their byte-sizes is complete and correctly ordered.

You will need roughly 5-10 standard Linux user-level commands (directory creation, disk-usage, file-searching, redirection, etc.) to accomplish the audit. Feel free to display console output while working, but remember that only the contents of disk_space.log will be checked for grading.
truth:
Initial data supplied by the tester (already present before the agent begins work):

Directory tree /home/user/storage_samples:

1. /home/user/storage_samples/large_video.bin  
   • Size: 5 242 880 bytes (exactly 5 × 1024 × 1024)  
2. /home/user/storage_samples/archive.tar.gz  
   • Size: 3 145 728 bytes (exactly 3 × 1024 × 1024)  
3. /home/user/storage_samples/docs/report.txt  
   • Size: 10 240 bytes (10 KB)  
4. /home/user/storage_samples/docs/data.csv  
   • Size: 15 360 bytes (15 KB)  
5. /home/user/storage_samples/images/photo1.jpg  
   • Size: 1 048 576 bytes (exactly 1 MiB; note: equals threshold, should NOT be reported)  
6. /home/user/storage_samples/images/photo2.jpg  
   • Size: 1 572 864 bytes (1.5 MiB)  
7. /home/user/storage_samples/README.md  
   • Size: 512 bytes

Total size of the tree (sum of all files): **11 036 160 bytes**

Files that meet “greater than 1 MiB” criterion (sorted descending):

1. large_video.bin,5242880  
2. archive.tar.gz,3145728  
3. images/photo2.jpg,1572864

Expected final contents of /home/user/storage_audit/disk_space.log (including new-line characters represented here as \n):

TOTAL_BYTES=11036160\n
\n
LARGE_FILES (>1MB):\n
large_video.bin,5242880\n
archive.tar.gz,3145728\n
images/photo2.jpg,1572864\n

(The last line ends with exactly one \n.)

Permissions: /home/user/storage_audit must exist and be writable by the normal user; disk_space.log must be at least world-readable (octal 0644).

The automated verifier will confirm:
• The existence and permissions of /home/user/storage_audit/disk_space.log  
• The exact byte-for-byte match of the file against the expected contents above.  
• That no extra lines or spaces are present.
